release_notes:
Alpha 5.0.77 - 2026-05-15 - Segfault diagnostics.

Pablo's user hit a Windows access-violation crash:
App finished (exit code: -1073741819)   (= 0xC0000005)
processing an ES SAP .xls file. App died at the C level so
Python never wrote a normal crash log -- we have zero
diagnostic info pointing at the offending C extension or line.

5.0.77 adds three defensive layers:

Layer 1 -- faulthandler in marosa_qt.py
----------------------------------------
New ``_enable_fault_handler()`` opens ``marosa_segfault.log``
next to the launcher and calls
``faulthandler.enable(file, all_threads=True)`` BEFORE
importing PySide6.

faulthandler installs OS signal handlers (SIGSEGV / SIGFPE /
SIGABRT / SIGBUS / SIGILL) that dump every thread's C+Python
stack to the log file BEFORE the process dies. ``all_threads=
True`` is critical because Pablo's user crashed in the
QThreadPool worker thread, not the UI thread.

Next crash, the user can attach marosa_segfault.log and we'll
have a real stack pointing at the offending C extension /
binding / line of code.

Layer 2 -- SAP worker outer except enriched
--------------------------------------------
src/qt_app/tabs/sap_tab.py::_SAPRunWorker.run had:
except Exception as exc:
self.signals.failed.emit(f"...{exc}")
The traceback was lost. 5.0.77:
except Exception as exc:
tb_text = traceback.format_exc()
self.signals.log.emit("✗ Worker crashed: ...\n{tb}")
self.signals.failed.emit(...)
except BaseException as exc:
self.signals.failed.emit(f"FATAL {...}")
raise
So Python-level crashes (the common case) get the full stack
in the run log, AND SystemExit / KeyboardInterrupt at least
surface a "FATAL" line before propagating. A true C segfault
bypasses Python so Layer 1 catches that.

Layer 3 -- START-HERE.txt section 5 names marosa_segfault.log
--------------------------------------------------------------
Trouble-launching section gains an explicit branch for the
-1073741819 / 0xC0000005 exit code: "this is a C-level
segfault, attach app/marosa_segfault.log when reporting it".
Plus the existing "Anything else" branch now lists BOTH
logs.

Tests
=====

tests/test_1008_qt_577_segfault_diag.py (NEW, 7):
- marosa_qt.py defines _enable_fault_handler.
- faulthandler.enable is called BEFORE the PySide6 import.
- Output stream points at marosa_segfault.log.
- all_threads=True is set (so worker-thread crashes get
dumped).
- SAP worker logs traceback.format_exc() on Exception.
- SAP worker has a BaseException catch after the Exception
one.
- START-HERE names marosa_segfault.log AND the
-1073741819 / 0xC0000005 exit code.

7/7 green.

What this does NOT fix
======================

It does NOT identify the offending C extension on Pablo's
user's machine. That requires the stack trace from the next
crash. Likely candidates (we'll narrow once we have the log):
- openpyxl C extension on a malformed xlsx
- xlrd 1.x crash on legacy .xls
- PySide6 cross-thread signal touching a deleted QObject
- pandas C-extension corner case
The faulthandler log will pin which one.

===============================================================
